text: The surgeon begins the core out fistulectomy, using a Lone Star retractor for deeper tracts. A microcautery device, such as the Colorado needle, is employed to remove granulation tissue up to the external sphincter. The cored out fistula is visible, often removed in one piece.

The surgeon performs a wide excision of the internal opening, creating a large elliptical defect to remove all separated tissue. The excised internal opening is visible, along with the de-epithelialized landing zone prepared for flap attachment. The external wound is temporarily packed to maintain hemostasis before suturing the internal opening.
The surgeon closes the defect at the internal opening using resorbable braided sutures after carefully clearing the intra-sphincteric space. The defect appears large following thorough debridement.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to create the advancement flap, ensuring quick cutting and effective hemostasis. The flap is approximately four to five centimeters in length, providing tension-free coverage below the sutured internal opening. A Lone Star retractor, augmented with a disposable plastic retractor, is used to maintain exposure during the procedure.

The surgeon advances and fixates the flap using two barbed sutures, incrementally securing it from proximal to distal. The flap is visibly well-fixed and well-perfused, providing good coverage over the sutured internal opening.
